content.full_text:
October 31, 1990


Glenn R. Watson
Richards, Watson & Gershon
Attorneys at Law
333 South Hope Street
Los Angeles, CA  90071-1469

Re:  Your Request for Assistance
Our File No. A-90-541

Dear Mr. Watson:

I am writing in response to your letter of August 13, 1990 to
Colleen McGee regarding your 1989 statement of economic interests.
As you were advised, your 1989 statement of economic interests
("SEI") does not comply with the requirements of the Political
Reform Act¹ in that it does not show your investment in your law
firm. In this regard, you have asked for a further explanation of
Section 82034 as it pertains to your situation.

Section 82034 defines investment, in pertinent part, as:

... any financial interest in or security issued by
a business entity, including but not limited to
common stock, preferred stock, rights, warrants,
options, debt instruments and any partnership or
other ownership interest owned directly, indirectly
or beneficially by the public official, or other
filer, or his or her immediate family, if the busi-
ness entity or any parent, subsidiary or otherwise
related business entity has an interest in real
property in the jurisdiction, or does business or
plans to do business in the jurisdiction, or has
done business within the jurisdiction at any time
during the two years prior to the time any state-
ment or other action is required under this title.
No asset shall be deemed an investment unless its
fair market value equals or exceeds one thousand
dollars ($1,000).


___________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-90-541
Page 2

Section 82025.5 defines fair market value as:

... the estimated fair market value of goods,
services, facilities or anything of value other
than money. Whenever the amount of goods,
services, facilities, or anything of value other
than money is required to be reported under this
title, the amount reported shall be the fair market
value, and a description of the goods, services,
facilities, or other thing of value shall be ap-
pended to the report or statement. "Full and
adequate consideration" as used in this title means
fair market value.

As you were advised, if you have an investment interest in
your law firm worth $1,000 or more, you must report this on
Schedule A--Investment of your SEI. You have stated that your
law firm is a corporation and each shareholder owns a certain
number of shares. The value of your shares is less than $900 and
the shares in the firm are not saleable. Therefore, you contend
that your investment interest is less than $1,000 and does not
have a fair market value.

However, the corporate structure of your firm does not limit
your investment interest to the value of your shares. Rather than
focus on the $900 value of your shares, which cannot be sold, a
better gauge of your investment interest is the value of the firm
and what you would receive upon its sale.

The definition of fair market value includes goods, services
and facilities. Your firm has assets, goodwill and a lease, all
of which can be sold or transferred. Surely, the value of any of
these must exceed the value of your shares. Therefore, as a named
partner in the firm, you have an ownership interest which exceeds
$1,000.

Accordingly, you have an investment interest in your law firm
over $1,000 which must be reported on Schedule A of your SEI.
Please contact Ms. Dixie Howard in the Technical Assistance and
Analysis Division at (916) 322-5662 to assist you in amending your
1989 SEI. If this amendment is not initiated within 30 days, this
matter will be referred to our Enforcement Division.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Jill K. Stecher
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JRS:plh

CC: Ms. Dixie Howard
